name: Bagan Shweshigon Pagoda
description: Ancient golden pagoda built in the 11th century, one of the most sacred Buddhist sites in Myanmar.
history: The Shwezigon Pagoda is a Buddhist stupa located in Nyaung-U, a town near Bagan, in Myanmar. It is a prototype of Burmese stupas, and consists of a circular gold leaf-gilded stupa surrounded by smaller temples and shrines. Construction of the Shwezigon Pagoda began during the reign of King Anawrahta (1044–1077), the founder of the Pagan Empire, in 1059–1060 and was completed in 1102, during the reign of his son King Kyansittha. Over the centuries, the pagoda had been damaged by many earthquakes and other natural calamities, and has been refurbished several times. In recent renovations, it has been covered by more than 30,000 copper plates. However, the lowest level terraces have remained as they were.

name: Shwedagon Pagoda
description: The most sacred Buddhist pagoda in Myanmar, covered in gold and adorned with thousands of diamonds.
history: The Shwedagon Pagoda, officially named Shwedagon Zedi Daw, also known as the Great Dagon Pagoda and the Golden Pagoda, is a gilded stupa located in Yangon, Myanmar. The 326-foot-tall pagoda is situated on Singuttara Hill, dominating the Yangon skyline. According to legend, the Shwedagon Pagoda has existed for more than 2,600 years, making it the oldest Buddhist stupa in the world. The stupa is completely covered in gold. It is plated in genuine gold, and the top of the stupa is encrusted with 4,531 diamonds; the largest of which is a 72-carat diamond. The gold seen on the stupa is made of genuine gold plates, attached to the surface of the stupa. People all over the country, as well as monarchs in its history, have donated gold to the pagoda to maintain it.
